scenario: Rails work session - audit the query Relation contract before a rework

map_quality.rationale: The step1 block correctly names Ruby on Rails as a monorepo, places ActiveRecord::Relation at activerecord/lib/active_record/relation.rb line 5, and lists the full relation/ subdirectory by file — enough for an agent to pick an entry point. The organization description ('each framework in its own directory') is correct but terse, and the lazy-execution point ('executed when records are loaded via to_sql/exec_queries') is present, so targeted follow-ups are small.

specificity.rationale: All shared pieces are named by full path (e.g. activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/spawn_methods.rb, relation/query_methods.rb) and the main file is pinned to line 5 — concrete enough for the agent to open the right file immediately without guessing.

justification.rationale: The answer states that the Relation 'accumulates query conditions through chaining and is executed when records are loaded' but never explains WHY the query interface is decomposed into mixed-in modules, WHY chaining defers execution, or WHY spawning a new relation on each chain call preserves immutability — the architectural reasoning the agent needs to change the contract safely is absent.

uncertainty.rationale: The step1 section flags no uncertainty at all — no hedges on how the layers connect, no notes on what was inferred vs. confirmed — leaving the agent no signal about where to verify before acting.

relationship.rationale: The answer states that 'finder/scope/association returns a Relation' and that it 'accumulates conditions through chaining,' which is the right claim, but the shared_pieces list is a bare file roster with no connecting relation stated (e.g. 'SpawnMethods is included into Relation to handle spawn/merge on each chain call' is absent); the agent cannot tell which files are mixed-in modules vs. standalone helpers vs. subclasses from the step1 text alone.

steps.step: Map the Relation contract and what it is built on

map_quality.rationale: The step2 block names all five canonical modules (SpawnMethods, QueryMethods, FinderMethods, Calculations, Delegation) plus Batches, SignedId::RelationMethods, and TokenFor::RelationMethods with file:line for each include site (relation.rb:68-71), every constant (VALUE_METHODS at :66, MULTI_VALUE_METHODS at :54, etc.), and key execution methods (to_sql :1243, exec_queries :1436). An agent knows exactly what the contract covers and can pick an entry point immediately; the only small gap is that it doesn't distinguish core preserved interface from optional addons.

specificity.rationale: Every contract piece carries a concrete path and line: 'SpawnMethods (activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/spawn_methods.rb:8)', 'Delegation (activerecord/lib/active_record/relation/delegation.rb:5)', constants at exact line numbers. Nothing is left at the vague 'query modules' level.

justification.rationale: The answer overwhelmingly lists methods per module rather than explaining WHY the design is shaped this way — there is no explanation that spawn/merge exist to keep chaining immutable, or that delegation is how model-class methods are routed through the Relation so callers never touch the model directly. The step1 'query_location' blurb mentions lazy accumulation but step2 itself does not carry that reasoning forward.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged anywhere — no 'did not open this method', no 'verify the delegation cache', no 'line numbers derived from grep, confirm before relying'. The answer is uniformly assertive.

relationship.rationale: The answer does state which module supplies which methods (partial relationship credit), and 'Modules included at line 68-71' identifies the include binding. But the key operational paths the rubric asks for are absent: how spawn clones the relation on chaining, how delegation routes through method_missing to the model class — a text search could produce the same method-per-module list, and the connecting mechanism is not stated.

steps.step: Trace how a query is built up and executed

map_quality.rationale: The answer covers creation points, chaining, spawn/merge (spawn_methods.rb:9/:33/:43), and execution (relation.rb:1243/:1436/:1460) but the path from SQL compilation through Arel to the database adapter is a thin stub ('arel - relation.rb - returns Arel::Nodes::SelectStatement' with no line), and the Relation::Merger is never traced through, leaving the agent with real follow-up work to close those gaps.

specificity.rationale: spawn_and_merge and execution entries carry file:line (e.g., spawn_methods.rb:9, relation.rb:1436), but most creation_points and all chaining_methods list only a filename without a line number, and the Merger class is referenced only implicitly via 'merge! - destructive merge using Relation::Merger' without its own file:line.

justification.rationale: The answer never explains WHY chaining spawns a new relation (immutability of an unexecuted relation) or WHY execution is deferred until enumeration; every entry describes WHAT the method does ('clones relation', 'generates SQL string') without the design rationale the rubric asks for.

uncertainty.rationale: There is no flagging of any uncertain step, no acknowledgment that the arel compilation path or Merger internals were not fully opened; the answer presents the entire path with uniform confidence despite clear gaps in line-level coverage.

relationship.rationale: The answer organizes content into separate lists (creation_points, chaining_methods, spawn_and_merge, execution) that are roughly ordered but never assembled into an explicit chain (finder/scope → spawn → merge conditions → compile via Arel → execute via adapter); the connecting route between each stage is absent, so the agent cannot follow the path without reconstructing the order itself.

steps.step: Audit every dependent of the Relation contract

map_quality.rationale: The answer covers the intra-gem call sites well (query modules, association subclasses, sanitization `===`, connection_handling, encryption prepend, signed_id/token_for RelationMethods, named scopes, join_dependency) but completely omits both required cross-gem dependents: the ActiveSupport Enumerable core-ext mirroring Relation#pick and the Railties query command rendering a relation as a table, which the rubric calls 'the heart of the ticket.'

specificity.rationale: For the intra-gem items the answer provides file:line and a one-phrase usage descriptor (e.g., 'sanitization.rb:214 — type check for subquery conversion', 'connection_handling.rb:419 — type check to determine loading strategy'), but the two cross-gem dependents have no entry at all, leaving a concrete gap rather than a vague one.

justification.rationale: Items are grouped by area (query modules, association subclasses, predicate building, cache keys, encryption, named scopes, connection execution) and each group carries a brief WHY phrase; however the reasoning is often just restatement of the mechanism ('monkey-patches Relation') without explaining what contract property makes it fragile, and the cross-gem WHY is entirely absent.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer contains no acknowledgment that static analysis misses dependents reaching Relation by short name, by a `===` type test that wouldn't surface under a class-name grep, or by cross-gem import — despite the rubric explicitly calling out this gap as the reason a class-name search fails on 388 of 401 files.

relationship.rationale: The answer does state the connecting mechanism for several items ('Relation === check', 'Relation.prepend(RelationQueries)', 'RelationMethods with find_signed'), but it never articulates the short-name / unqualified-reference path that links most intra-gem call sites to the Relation contract, and provides no path at all for the cross-gem dependents whose relationship (mirror/render) is precisely what makes them non-obvious.

steps.step: Trace how relation behavior is reused for associations

map_quality.rationale: The answer names CollectionProxy at `collection_proxy.rb:31`, AssociationRelation at `association_relation.rb:4`, and AssociationScope at `association_scope.rb:6`, giving an agent entry points. However, the step5 section is thin: it gives only class-definition line numbers with no detail on where within those files the delegation mechanism lives, how scope is actually built, or how a chained call flows from CollectionProxy through to query execution.

specificity.rationale: File:line references are present for the top-level class definitions (`collection_proxy.rb:31`, `association_scope.rb:6`, `association_relation.rb:4`) but no method-level lines are provided — the agent cannot immediately locate `AssociationScope#scope`, the delegation hook in CollectionProxy, or `exec_queries` override in AssociationRelation without further reads.

justification.rationale: The answer states that CollectionProxy 'subclasses Relation and delegates to association's relation via delegation cache' but gives no explanation of WHY this design exists — nothing about why associations need the same chainable query interface or why subclassing was chosen over composition.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags no uncertainty whatsoever; there is no acknowledgment that the 'delegation cache' mechanism was not fully opened, that CollectionProxy's internal forwarding to `@association.scope` was not verified, or that DisableJoins path was not traced end-to-end.

relationship.rationale: The `relation_reuse_patterns` list sketches that CollectionProxy subclasses Relation and that scopes return Relations via `AssociationRelation.create`, but the connecting chain — user calls `post.comments.where(...)` → CollectionProxy → delegates to association's scope (an AssociationRelation) → AssociationScope builds the query → merged into Relation — is not assembled as an explicit path; 'delegation cache' is a vague label with no path expressed.

steps.step: Assess the blast radius of the contract change

map_quality.rationale: The answer covers intra-activerecord dependents reasonably (query modules, subclasses, sanitization, encryption, scoping) but entirely omits the cross-gem dependents the rubric flags as critical: activesupport's Enumerable core-ext that mirrors Relation#pick and the Railties query command that renders a relation as a table — exactly the area a hand audit of the query directory misses. connection_handling.rb's role in execution under a role/shard is absent from the ranked list despite appearing in step4.

specificity.rationale: Most high-risk entries carry file:line and a one-phrase reason (e.g., sanitization.rb:214 "Relation === check in bind variable replacement"), but medium/low entries often lack precise line numbers and the cross-gem files are never named at all, leaving a structural gap in the map.

justification.rationale: The answer labels risk tiers but rarely explains the mechanism that determines the tier — "any changes to base Relation constructor would break" describes the outcome, not WHY AssociationRelation is high while signed_id is low (one overrides exec_queries on the changed path; the other only extends the already-executed result set). The distinction between spawns/merges/executes a relation vs merely renders a loaded one is not drawn.

uncertainty.rationale: There is no acknowledgment that unqualified short-name references or cross-gem dependents could be missed by a static pass, no flag that the encryption prepend could surface surprises, and no instruction for the agent to verify anything it could not confirm — the answer reads as complete and settled.

relationship.rationale: Connections are stated as labels ("Subclass overriding", "monkey-patching") rather than paths from the Relation change to the dependent; the sanitization `===` type test is the closest to an explicit route but is not connected to the spawn/merge/execute change that triggers it. No entry expresses the chain as 'Relation.spawn change → AssociationRelation.exec_queries breaks' or 'Relation execute → sanitization inlines via === check', and the cross-gem paths (Enumerable mirror, Railties render) are absent entirely.

map_quality.rationale: The map covers the core activerecord edit surface well (relation.rb, spawn_methods.rb, query_methods.rb, association subclasses, encryption, scoping, predicate_builder) and a solid test list, but critically omits the cross-gem dependents the rubric foregrounds — the ActiveSupport Enumerable core-ext mirroring Relation#pick and the Railties query command — and drops sanitization.rb and connection_handling.rb from the edit list despite explicitly naming them in step4; no file:line annotations appear in files_to_edit or tests_to_update, so a teammate would still need to locate insertion points.

specificity.rationale: File names are concrete but stripped of line numbers throughout the step7 lists (in contrast to steps 2–6 where file:line was present), the cross-gem scope (activesupport/.../core_ext/enumerable.rb, railties/.../commands/query/query_command.rb) is entirely absent, and sanitization.rb — cited at line 214 in step4 — never appears in the edit map.

justification.rationale: Both files_to_edit and tests_to_update are bare lists with no explanation of why each entry is in the map or what the Relation change does to it; the rubric asks for a 'stated reason' per file and 'which area it belongs to,' none of which is present.

uncertainty.rationale: Step7 contains zero uncertainty flags — no caveat about the cross-gem dependents being the least-verified part, no note about spawn/merge semantics needing confirmation, no flag on fixtures; the omission means an agent acting on this map would proceed without knowing which parts are underconfident.

relationship.rationale: The map is a grouped file list with no connecting route stated per entry — nothing like 'sanitization.rb via the `=== Relation` type test' or 'activesupport enumerable via Relation#pick mirroring' — which the rubric scores explicitly low regardless of how complete the file list is.
